elements.sent-2411-149: Fragility \cite{wang2023data, asikuzzaman2017overview, langelaar2000watermarking in watermarking describes the sensitivity of the watermark to various alterations or attacks on the watermarked content, enabling verification of content integrity and detection of unauthorized modifications.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Fragile watermarks are particularly useful in contexts where authenticity and tamper detection are critical, as they reveal alterations by failing to withstand even minor changes to the host data.
elements.sent-2411-151: Semi-fragile watermark allows for slight modifications to host data within a certain range, which can tolerate some non-malicious operations, such as compression, slight noise, and brightness adjustments, without becoming invalid due to these minor changes.
elements.sent-2411-152: Fragility is inherently opposite to robustness, as fragile watermarks are not designed to survive typical attacks or transformations.
elements.sent-2411-153: The choice between fragility and robustness ultimately depends on the application’s primary goals.
elements.sent-2411-154: In environments where content integrity is critical, fragility is more advantageous as it reveals even minor alterations.
elements.sent-2411-155: Efficiency \cite{begum2020digital, agarwal2019survey, anand2021watermarking in watermarking refers to the computational speed and resource requirements involved in embedding and extracting the watermark from the multimedia content.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Efficiency is influenced by factors, e.g., the watermarking algorithm’s complexity, the data size of the host media, and the hardware used for processing.
elements.sent-2411-157: A high degree of efficiency may sometimes conflict with the robustness and fidelity of the watermark.
elements.sent-2411-158: For instance, complex algorithms may offer higher resistance to attacks but require more processing power and time, impacting efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-159: Conversely, simpler algorithms may lack robustness against sophisticated attacks.
elements.sent-2411-160: In summary, achieving optimal efficiency in watermarking requires a careful balance between computational demands and robustness, tailored to the application’s specific requirements.
elements.sent-2411-161: Capacity \cite{aberna2024digital, begum2020digital, agarwal2019survey in watermarking refers to the amount of information that can be embedded within the multimedia content, often quantified as bit-per-pixel in images or bit-per-frame in videos.}
elements.sent-2411-162: Capacity is not only a measure of data quantity but also impacts the scheme’s overall functionality in applications requiring high distinguishability between multiple watermarked instances.
elements.sent-2411-163: For instance, the classical watermarking algorithm, Least Significant Bit (LSB) \cite{kalker1999video,chopra2012lsb embeds data by altering the least significant bits of pixel values in an image, without causing noticeable distortion to the human eye.}
elements.sent-2411-164: It is important to balance capacity with other watermark properties, as increasing capacity often risks reducing the watermark’s stealthiness or robustness.
elements.sent-2411-165: Consequently, watermark capacity must be carefully optimized alongside other performance metrics to ensure the watermark remains unobtrusive and resilient in its intended application environment.
elements.sent-2411-166: Invertibility \cite{wang2023data, begum2020digital, mohanarathinam2020digital in watermarking, also known as reversibility, refers to the capability of a watermarking scheme to allow the embedded watermark to be removed from the watermarked content, enabling complete recovery of the original content.}
elements.sent-2411-167: Invertibility guarantees not only the watermark’s full extraction but also the exact reconstruction of the multimedia content.
elements.sent-2411-168: Formally, a watermarking scheme is defined as invertible if: \begin{equation}D(E(x,m))=(x,m)\end{equation} where $D(E(x, m))$ represents the result of fully extracting $m$ and restoring $x$ without any residual alterations.
elements.sent-2411-169: Although invertibility enhances authenticity and content integrity, it may present a trade-off with other properties such as robustness.
elements.sent-2411-170: If robustness is increased, achieving precise invertibility becomes more difficult, as the scheme must handle both extraction and full reconstruction of the original content after enduring potential distortions.
elements.sent-2411-171: Blindness \cite{wang2023data, jiao2019review, wan2022comprehensive in watermarking refers to how much information is required to extract the original data from the encoded media.}
elements.sent-2411-172: Specifically, a blind watermarking scheme enables the extraction of the embedded watermark without requiring access to the original cover media, making it highly practical for real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-173: Semi-blind techniques require only the original data, whereas Non-blind techniques require both the original cover media and data in order to perform data extraction.
elements.sent-2411-174: It is necessary to detect and extract the message without the original media.
elements.sent-2411-175: Consequently, blindness not only increases the practicality of the watermarking scheme but also supports its reliability in diverse applications where the original media cannot feasibly be retained or accessed.
elements.sent-2411-176: Uniqueness \cite{ma2023generative ensures each watermark is distinct to a specific content creator or user, allowing accurate identification without overlap.}
elements.sent-2411-177: Uniqueness is measured by the detector’s ability to differentiate its own watermarked outputs from those of other users.
elements.sent-2411-178: Formally, let $\mathcal{D _i$ and $\mathcal{D _j$ be detectors associated with subjects $i$ and $j$, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-179: A watermarking scheme achieves uniqueness if: \begin{equation}\Pr(\mathcal{D}_i(y'_i) = \text{true}) \gg \Pr(\mathcal{D}_j(y'_i) = \text{true}) \quad \forall i \neq j\end{equation} where $y'_i$ is the watermarked content uniquely assigned to subject $i$.
elements.sent-2411-180: This ensures that each detector $\mathcal{D _i$ accurately identifies its own watermarked content, thus preventing cross-user detection or confusion and maintaining secure ownership attribution.}
elements.sent-2411-181: AIGC watermarks are inherently designed to be embedded within the content generation process, providing a seamless and robust means of authenticating and tracing content.
elements.sent-2411-182: Unlike traditional watermarks that often serve as external add-ons, AIGC watermarks integrate directly into the generative pipeline, offering unique attributes that enhance security, flexibility, and cross-media applicability.
elements.sent-2411-183: Key properties of AIGC watermarking, including endogeneity, cross-modal attributes, plug-and-play capability, and accessibility, address the needs of AI-driven applications, where security, authenticity, and ease of verification are paramount.
elements.sent-2411-184: Endogeneity in AIGC watermarking refers to the intrinsic relationship between the watermark and the supply chain itself.
elements.sent-2411-185: Unlike traditional watermarks that are typically added as a post-processing step, endogenous watermarks are inherently tied to the generation process.
elements.sent-2411-186: This tight integration enhances security by making watermarks harder to remove without degrading content quality, while also improving efficiency by eliminating separate watermarking steps.
elements.sent-2411-187: Formally, let $T$ be a supply chain.
elements.sent-2411-188: Given data sources $X$, traditional watermarking can be expressed as: \begin{equation}y' = \mathcal{W}(T(X))\end{equation} where $y'$ is the watermarked output, and the watermark is applied after generation.
elements.sent-2411-189: In contrast, endogenous watermarking is integrated into the supply chain itself, which can be represented as: \begin{equation}y' = T_w(X)\end{equation} where $T_w$ is the watermark-aware process that directly generates watermarked content.
elements.sent-2411-190: For instance, this integration can be achieved by modifying the parameters of a generative model $v_i$ in the supply chain.
elements.sent-2411-191: Consequently, the watermarking function becomes an integral part of the model's parameters $\theta_w$: \begin{equation}\theta_w = f(\theta, \mathcal{W})\end{equation} where $\theta$ represents the original model parameters and $f$ is a function that integrates the watermarking scheme into the model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-192: Cross-modal attributes in AIGC watermarking refer to the capability of embedding watermarks across various content formats or media types generated by the model.
elements.sent-2411-193: This approach contrasts with traditional watermarks that are typically restricted to a single medium, enabling cohesive authentication across images, text, audio, and other content types within a unified framework.
elements.sent-2411-194: This cross-modal consistency increases the difficulty of removing or altering the watermark, as it would require a multi-faceted adjustment across diverse formats, thus reinforcing overall security.
elements.sent-2411-195: The ability to sustain watermark integrity across media types not only simplifies the verification process but also enhances robustness against tampering, making it highly suitable for applications demanding seamless verification across varied content formats.
elements.sent-2411-196: The plug-and-play capability in AIGC watermarking allows watermark embedding without retraining or fine-tuning the generative model.
elements.sent-2411-197: This is particularly valuable for large models, where retraining is computationally intensive.
elements.sent-2411-198: Plug-and-play frameworks integrate seamlessly into existing generative pipelines as auxiliary modules, thereby embedding watermarks while preserving the model’s original parameters.
elements.sent-2411-199: Formally, let $v_i$ represent the generative model with parameters $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-200: In plug-and-play watermarking, the output $y'$ is generated as: \begin{equation}y' = \mathcal{W}(v_i(x, \theta))\end{equation} where $\mathcal{W}$ operates independently of $\theta$, so the generative process $v_i(x, \theta)$ remains unaffected by the watermarking mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-201: This isolation of $\mathcal{W}$ from $\theta$ increases the adversarial difficulty, as bypassing or altering $\mathcal{W}$ would necessitate tampering with an external component rather than the model’s core parameters.
elements.sent-2411-202: Accessibility in AIGC watermarking defines the watermark’s security, ensuring that only authorized users can verify it.
elements.sent-2411-203: In some AIGC watermarking techniques, access to specific internal components, such as token logits from the language model API and the original prompt, is required to identify the watermark within generated content, to ensure that watermark verification is controlled and secure.
elements.sent-2411-204: An accessible watermarking scheme requires that the watermark be detectable only if: \begin{equation}m' = m \; \quad \text{if and only if} \; \quad \text{id} \in \mathcal{A}\end{equation} where $\mathcal{A}$ is the set of authorized ids required for watermark detection.
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}m' = m \; \quad if and only if \; \quad id \in A\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-205: This restriction makes it challenging for unauthorized users to detect or manipulate the watermark, thus securing the watermark’s integrity.
elements.sent-2411-206: In this section, we recap the AIGC watermarking techniques from the functionality side and provide the formal definitions of these functionalities.
elements.sent-2411-207: The functionalities of the AIGC watermark can be summarized as five aspects, including detection, attribution, copyright protection, steganography, and tamper detection.
elements.sent-2411-208: We can further refine the functionalities according to the five elements (i.e., dataset, user, generative model, AIGC, and watermark) in the AIGC watermarking.
elements.sent-2411-210: Common properties required for AIGC watermarking are stealthiness, soundness, and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-211: One of the key functionalities of watermarking technology is to efficiently and accurately detect AIGC, determining whether a particular information media has been generated by AI.
elements.sent-2411-212: AIGC is challenging to distinguish through human judgment \cite{lu2024seeing with high deceptiveness, leading to various illegal activities, such as AI-enabled fraud and public opinion manipulation.}
elements.sent-2411-213: This section categorizes methods for enabling AIGC detection in watermarking technology into two types: detection by watermarks embedded during content generation and detection by watermarks embedded directly into the generated content.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{functionality.pdf} \caption{The overview of the functionalities of AIGC watermarking.} \label{fig:functionality}
elements.subsubsection-2411-016: Detection by watermarks embedded during content generation
elements.sent-2411-214: This kind of method identifies AIGC by embedding detectable features within the generated outputs during the model's generation phase \cite{zeng2023securing,kirchenbauer2023watermark,liu2019novel,fang2023flow,wang2023free,zhao2023provable,xiong2023flexible .}
elements.sent-2411-215: This approach focuses on embedding watermarks into the nodes other than the root node $r$ in the supply chain, typically by modifying the model parameters \cite{zeng2023securing,kirchenbauer2023watermark or altering the training dataset \cite{bui2023rosteals,luo2022leca .}}
elements.sent-2411-216: By doing so, the model's outputs inherently carry these embedded markers, which serve as identifiable signatures for detection.
elements.sent-2411-217: Specifically, during the training process, targeted interventions, such as adjusting weights or introducing specialized training samples, encode detectable and uniquely identifiable features into the outputs.
elements.sent-2411-218: These features can then be extracted during the detection phase, allowing the identification of AIGC based on the presence or absence of these embedded watermarks.
elements.sent-2411-219: The core idea is to ensure that any content generated by the model contains detectable markers, making it possible to reliably identify AIGC.
elements.subsubsection-2411-017: Detection by watermarks embedded directly to the generated content
elements.sent-2411-220: This kind of method often employs a separate watermarking system to process and embed detectable features directly into the AIGC, i.e., the root node $r$ in the supply chain~\cite{singh2017dwt,yang2022tracing,tan2024waterdiff,luo2020distortion,ahmadi2020redmark .}
elements.sent-2411-221: This reduces the reliance of detection methods on the generative model itself, providing greater flexibility and independence.
elements.sent-2411-222: Although this approach decouples the detection process from model training, it can still utilize generative AI to enhance the quality of watermarked AIGC and improve detection robustness.
elements.sent-2411-223: For example, replacing traditional CNN-based detection methods with GANs \cite{wen2019romark,liu2017coverless allows post-processing methods to benefit from enhanced generative capabilities, thereby refining the detection process.}
elements.sent-2411-224: The watermark inside the AIGC can also signify its origin.
elements.sent-2411-225: Therefore, AIGC watermarks have the capability named attribution (or traceability) to help trace the origin of the AIGC.
elements.sent-2411-226: Based on the target, attributions can be divided into model attribution and user attribution.
elements.sent-2411-227: To achieve multi-model or multi-user attribution, AIGC watermarking for attribution needs adequate capability and robustness.
elements.sent-2411-228: Model attribution aims to identify the generative model responsible for specific content~\cite{liu2024model .}
elements.sent-2411-229: By embedding watermarks within models, the watermark can later be extracted from generated content to verify the model’s origin \cite{zhou2024traceablespeech, lukas2023ptw, fei2022supervised .}
elements.sent-2411-230: For image models, Lukas et al. \cite{lukas2023ptw proposed fine-tuning pre-trained generators to produce watermarked images.}
elements.sent-2411-231: On the other hand, Fei et al.~\cite{fei2022supervised insert a pre-trained watermarking decoding block at the output of the GAN generator to achieve model attribution.}
elements.sent-2411-232: For text models, Baldassini et al. \cite{baldassini2024cross utilized cross-attention mechanisms to embed watermarks in large language models (LLMs), introducing methods that minimally impact performance.}
elements.sent-2411-233: Qu et al. \cite{qu2024provably proposed segment-based watermarking with error correction for robust text watermarking.}
elements.sent-2411-234: In multi-modal models, Zhou et al. \cite{zhou2024traceablespeech focused on embedding watermarks to text-to-speech models so that synthetic speech output by the models will contain a watermark.}
elements.sent-2411-235: While the aforementioned studies have provided some initial insights into the issue of model attribution, it is important to acknowledge that a comprehensive solution to model attribution remains unresolved.
elements.sent-2411-236: Furthermore, there is an urgent need for research on different modal models, such as audio, in order to address this gap in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-237: User attribution identifies the individual using AI to generate content.
elements.sent-2411-238: Unlike model attribution, which employs a fixed watermarking module, user attribution requires a flexible system that embeds user-specific messages during generation.
elements.sent-2411-239: One common approach is fine-tuning models into distinct versions \cite{fernandez2023stable , where each version generates content with a different watermark for various users.}
elements.sent-2411-240: For instance, Fernandez et al. fine-tuned the VAE decoder of latent diffusion models with a pre-trained watermark decoder and fixed watermark message.
elements.sent-2411-241: Kim et al. \cite{kim2024wouaf modified generative models using the distributor's unique digital fingerprint through weight modulation.}
elements.sent-2411-242: However, such approaches can be costly when many users are involved since the overhead of fine-tuning a model is substantial.
elements.sent-2411-243: To tackle the above limitation, a promising solution involves adding a plug-and-play watermarking module that can embed user-specific watermarks during generation.
elements.sent-2411-244: Feng et al. \cite{feng2023catch introduced Concept Watermarking to trace malicious users generating illegal images, embedding watermark information into the target concept.}
elements.sent-2411-245: GROOT \cite{liu2024groot encodes watermarks into the latent space for audio synthesis.}
elements.sent-2411-246: Overall, flexible watermarking modules can enhance user attribution, enabling more effective accountability in content generation.
elements.sent-2411-247: Copyright protection is a fundamental functionality of digital watermarks.
elements.sent-2411-248: By extracting watermarks from digital assets, the owner can verify their ownership.
elements.sent-2411-249: AIGC watermarking can also be used to protect the copyright of various components in AI, such as datasets, models, and AIGC.
elements.sent-2411-250: Dataset copyright protection focuses on safeguarding artifacts and datasets from unauthorized use, particularly in training generative models~\cite{li2022untargeted, guo2023domain, li2023black .}
elements.sent-2411-251: Although some cutting-edge works have explored the issue of dataset copyright protection~\cite{wei2024pointncbw, guo2024zero, he2024difficulty , they are primarily focused on classification models and fail to directly apply to generative models due to the inherent differences in the output formats between generative models and classification models.}
elements.sent-2411-252: As such, AIGC watermarking stands as a promising solution to dataset copyright protection for generative models.
elements.sent-2411-254: First, the owner embeds a specific watermark into their artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-255: When these artifacts are used to train a model, the model produces watermarked content.
elements.sent-2411-256: By extracting the watermark from the generated content, the owner can verify whether the model developer has infringed upon their copyright \cite{ye2024duaw, cui2023diffusionshield, zhu2024watermark .}
elements.sent-2411-257: For instance, SIREN~\cite{li2025towards proposes to optimize the watermark perturbation in a way to be recognized by the model as a feature relevant to the personalization task.}
elements.sent-2411-258: For text generation models, WATERFALL \cite{lau2024protecting is a robust and scalable watermarking framework that operates without requiring training.}
elements.sent-2411-259: It integrates a large language model (LLM) paraphraser to embed watermarks in text, ensuring that the watermarks remain verifiable even after paraphrasing, translation, or use in LLM training.
elements.sent-2411-260: Model copyright protection refers to determining the ownership of a suspicious model~\cite{adi2018turning, shao2025explanation .}
elements.sent-2411-261: Unlike traditional model watermarking methods that conduct ownership verification through testing specific input-output pairs~\cite{li2022defending, yang2023watermarking, shao2024fedtracker , AIGC watermarking for model copyright protection involves extracting the watermark only from the generated contents~\cite{wu2020watermarking, yang2024gaussian, zhao2023recipe .}}
elements.sent-2411-262: A typical solution to AIGC-watermarking-based model copyright protection is to utilize a specific watermark decoder during training.
elements.sent-2411-263: The watermark decoder can extract the watermark from the AIGC and this watermarking objective is formulated into the training loss function.
elements.sent-2411-264: For instance, Zhang et al. \cite{zhang2020model proposed embedding invisible watermarks into outputs of image processing models, enabling attackers to learn the watermark when training surrogate models.}
elements.sent-2411-265: Wu et al. \cite{wu2020watermarking developed a joint training approach for host and watermark-extraction networks, achieving a low bit error rate but at a high cost.}
elements.sent-2411-266: Peng et al.~\cite{peng2023intellectual introduced WDM for diffusion models, which encodes watermarks during task generation.}
elements.sent-2411-267: Building on the prior works, a few existing works also provide concrete steps to improve the robustness or stealthiness of the AIGC watermarking for model copyright protection~\cite{zhang2024suppressing, liu2023watermarking .}
elements.sent-2411-268: AIGC copyright protection involves legal measures that safeguard the rights of the creators of AIGC.
elements.sent-2411-269: Existing AIGC watermarking techniques (e.g., the watermarking methods for detection or attribution)~\cite{kirchenbauer2023watermark, wen2023tree are instrumental in AIGC copyright protection.}
elements.sent-2411-270: By embedding unique identifiers in generated content, watermarking asserts ownership and facilitates tracing of the work's origin.
elements.sent-2411-271: These watermarks can serve as legal evidence, deterring infringement and ensuring the integrity of AIGC against unauthorized modifications.
elements.sent-2411-272: Steganography focuses on the concealment of secret information within the generated content.
elements.sent-2411-273: In AIGC, steganography enables secure, undetectable embedding of information across multiple modalities, preserving content quality and supporting applications needing confidentiality, authentication, or traceability.
elements.sent-2411-274: The key properties of steganography are capacity and stealthiness.
elements.sent-2411-275: Text-based steganography in AIGC embeds secret information within the generated text, enabling secure data transfer.
elements.sent-2411-276: Methods involve manipulating text features, leveraging natural language patterns, and using advanced LLMs for better capacity and concealment.
elements.sent-2411-277: Early LSTM and RNN approaches \cite{fang2017generating,yang2018rnn struggled with extraction accuracy and robustness.}
elements.sent-2411-278: Liu et al. \cite{liu2024generative used a ResNet-LSTM to embed information in live comments, achieving high stealth but limited.}
elements.sent-2411-279: Recent LLM-based methods like LLM-Stega \cite{wu2024generative and PPLM-Stega \cite{cao2022generative also enhance capacity and text quality.}}
elements.sent-2411-280: Image-based steganography in AIGC enhances the capacity and security of hidden information within images, often using Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs).
elements.sent-2411-281: Hayes and Danezis \cite{hayes2017generating embedded secret data into images during generation via adversarial training, achieving robustness against detection tools.}
elements.sent-2411-282: Zhang et al. \cite{zhang2019steganogan introduced SteganoGAN, hiding data within images using an encoder, a decoder, and critic networks, attaining high embedding rates up to 4.4 bits per pixel while maintaining low detection rates.}
elements.sent-2411-283: Zhang et al. \cite{zhang2023steganography exploited generative model volatility in black-box scenarios to enhance security.}
elements.sent-2411-284: Audio and video steganography embed secret information within auditory and visual media, enabling secure and imperceptible communication.
elements.sent-2411-285: In audio steganography, methods include optimizing resource efficiency, using generative adversarial networks (GANs) for cover audio generation, and coverless approaches that generate steganographic audio directly from secret messages.
elements.sent-2411-286: For instance, Jiang et al. \cite{jiang2020smartsteganogaphy proposed SmartSteganography, a lightweight model for IoT applications that efficiently embeds messages while maintaining audio quality.}
elements.sent-2411-287: Chen et al. \cite{chen2021learning developed a GAN-based framework generating high-quality cover audio resistant to steganalysis, while Li et al. \cite{li2023coverless introduced a coverless method using WaveGAN to enhance concealment.}}
elements.sent-2411-288: Video steganography leverages temporal and spatial information in videos to conceal data within semantic attributes of content.
elements.sent-2411-289: Mao et al. \cite{mao2024covert presented the Robust Generative Video Steganography Network (RoGVSN), encoding secret information into features like facial attributes with high extraction accuracy under distortion attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-290: Tamper detection can be broadly categorized into two aspects: determining whether a watermark-protected sample has been altered and identifying the specific regions within the sample that have undergone modifications.
elements.sent-2411-291: Different from other functionalities, tamper detection depends on the fragility of AIGC watermarking.
elements.sent-2411-292: The primary objective at the sample level is to ascertain the integrity of the entire content by detecting any unauthorized modifications.
elements.sent-2411-293: Common methods for sample-level tamper detection include embedding robust adversarial watermarks, such as Cmua-watermark and Robust Invisible Watermarking (RIW), to ensure that any unauthorized modifications in the sample can be detected effectively.
elements.sent-2411-294: Huang et al.~\cite{huang2022cmua introduce Cmua-watermark, a cross-model universal adversarial watermark designed to disrupt tampered images generated by AI.}
elements.sent-2411-295: By embedding adversarial watermarks, their approach ensures that any tampering attempts by deepfake technologies can be effectively identified.
elements.sent-2411-296: Similarly, Tan et al.~\cite{tan2023somewhat propose Robust Invisible Watermarking (RIW), which leverages adversarial example techniques to embed watermarks that remain extractable even after diffusion-based editing.}
elements.sent-2411-297: Their experimental results demonstrate a high extraction accuracy of 96\% post-editing, significantly outperforming traditional methods.
elements.sent-2411-298: These approaches emphasize the importance of embedding robust watermarks that can reliably indicate the integrity of the entire sample.
elements.sent-2411-299: Beyond merely detecting tampering, it is crucial to pinpoint the exact regions within a sample that have been altered.
elements.sent-2411-300: Methods for localizing tampered regions commonly involve embedding dual invisible watermarks.
elements.sent-2411-301: Zhang et al.~\cite{zhang2024editguard present EditGuard, a versatile watermarking framework that embeds dual invisible watermarks to enable precise localization of tampered areas alongside copyright protection.}
elements.sent-2411-302: EditGuard utilizes a joint image-bit steganography network (IBSN).
elements.sent-2411-303: Building on this, Zhang et al.~\cite{zhang2024v2a introduce V2A-Mark, which extends watermarking to visual-audio content.}
elements.sent-2411-304: Another solution is to employ robust invertible steganography to preserve watermark integrity, enabling accurate detection of altered areas.
elements.sent-2411-305: Xu et al.~\cite{xu2022robust develop Robust Invertible Image Steganography (RIIS), which employs a conditional normalizing flow to model high-frequency image components, thereby enhancing the robustness and imperceptibility of hidden watermarks.}
elements.sent-2411-306: Such a method collectively advance the capability to not only detect tampering but also accurately identify and localize the modified regions within watermarked content.
elements.subsection-2411-015: Future Work on Functionality of AIGC Watermarking
elements.sent-2411-307: The field of AIGC watermarking presents several avenues for future research aimed at enhancing security and usability, as follows.
elements.sent-2411-308: Future research may emphasize the property of public accessibility and the creation of publicly detectable watermarks that enable third-party verification of the origin and authenticity of generated content.
elements.sent-2411-309: Existing works tend to require certain prior knowledge of the embedded watermark (e.g., the watermark decoder or extractor) when extracting the watermark.
elements.sent-2411-310: Developing a publicly detectable AIGC watermarking method aims to ensure that the watermarks can be easily identified by external parties and the public, without necessitating proprietary detection tools.
elements.sent-2411-311: This approach may improve the transparency of AIGC watermarking.
elements.subsubsection-2411-029: Protecting AIGC from Unauthorized Usage for Training
elements.sent-2411-312: Protecting AIGC from unauthorized usage for training purposes may also be a crucial area for future exploration while Existing methods mainly focus on the protection of artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-313: Research may focus on developing methods to identify and restrict the use of AIGC in subsequent nodes in the supply chain, such as training datasets.
elements.sent-2411-314: This may help better safeguard the copyright of AIGC and avoid unsafe usage.
elements.subsubsection-2411-030: Tamper Detection of Model and Dataset via AIGC Watermarking
elements.sent-2411-315: Future work may also address the integration of tamper detection mechanisms for both models and datasets through AIGC watermarking while existing methods only pay attention to AIGC tamper detection.
elements.sent-2411-316: By embedding unique identifiers or verification codes within model architectures or training datasets, researchers could facilitate the tracking of unauthorized modifications or alterations.
elements.sent-2411-317: This enhancement would enhance the overall integrity of AIGC systems, providing a reliable means of verifying the legitimacy of both the models and the data used for training.
elements.sent-2411-318: In this section, we first derive the detailed attack surfaces of the AIGC watermark based on the threat model defined in \cref{sec:problem:threat_model .}
elements.sent-2411-319: After that, we introduce the existing attack on the AIGC watermark.
elements.sent-2411-320: Finally, we discuss the potential directions for future research on the security side of the AIGC watermark.
elements.sent-2411-321: Recent research \cite{jiang2023evading, zhao2023invisible indicates that watermarks exhibit vulnerability when faced with attacks.}
elements.sent-2411-322: In \cref{sec:problem:threat_model , we briefly introduced the attack surfaces related to watermarking in AIGC.}
elements.sent-2411-323: Here, we delve deeper to gain a better understanding of the relationship between the AIGC watermarking process, the attack surfaces, and the corresponding attack methods.
elements.sent-2411-324: Manipulating $E$ could result in watermark detection attacks, as it plays a crucial role in the watermark extraction process.
elements.sent-2411-325: Adversaries can search for the watermark's presence, and if it is absent, they can use the AIGC outputs freely, resulting in the misuse of AIGC technologies.
elements.sent-2411-326: The vulnerability of the watermark inserting function $v'_e$ makes it susceptible to watermark forgery attacks since it is responsible for the watermark embedding process.
elements.sent-2411-327: If adversaries successfully exploit this weakness, they can create counterfeit watermarks that mimic the original.
elements.sent-2411-328: This allows the adversary to falsely attribute AIGC content to the victim, potentially leading to defamation and damage to the victim's reputation.
elements.sent-2411-329: The manipulation of $x_i$, $v_i$ and $o_i'$ can be grouped together as they occur in the stage where the watermark has been embedded but not yet extracted.
elements.sent-2411-330: These attack surfaces are particularly susceptible to watermark removal attacks.
elements.sent-2411-331: Attackers can adjust model parameters, alter the model structures, or transform outputs to evade the watermark from generated content.
elements.sent-2411-332: Once the watermark is removed, attackers can exploit AIGC content freely, resulting in disseminating false information and infringing on others' intellectual property rights.
elements.sent-2411-333: These attacks raise concerns about the reliability of watermarking as a protective mechanism, emphasizing the importance of developing more robust and resilient watermarking solutions.
elements.sent-2411-334: In watermark detection, attackers aim to detect if the content contains any hidden watermark.
elements.sent-2411-335: Watermark detection attacks specifically target the watermark extractor function $E$, seeking to identify and exploit vulnerabilities in how watermarks are embedded and retrieved.
elements.sent-2411-336: As far as we know, there are currently only two works on watermark detection.
elements.sent-2411-337: One approach leverages offset learning to isolate the influence of watermarks by comparing watermarked and non-watermarked datasets, allowing for more precise identification of the embedded watermark signals \cite{pan2024finding .}
elements.sent-2411-338: Another method finds that Enhancing feature extraction for steganographic image detection further improves the ability to detect watermarks, outperforming traditional CNN models \cite{tan2014stacked .}
elements.sent-2411-339: Given the lack of work in this area, it is essential that more research on watermark detection is conducted to advance this field.
elements.sent-2411-340: Watermark forgery involves adversaries forging a watermark, making the vanilla content with the forged watermark verified as the watermarked content.
elements.sent-2411-341: These attacks specifically target the watermark embedding function $v'_e$ as the attack surface.
elements.sent-2411-342: In text watermark forgery, techniques often concentrate on analyzing token distributions or utilizing the inherent characteristics of large language models, whereas image watermark forgery generally involves using datasets of source images and watermarked images for adversarial training.
elements.sent-2411-343: For text watermark forgery, one approach focuses on analyzing token distributions in large language model responses, allowing attackers to approximate watermarking rules and successfully spoof or manipulate watermarks, achieving high success rates \cite{jovanovic2024watermark .}
elements.sent-2411-344: Besides, attackers can exploit the inherent properties of watermarking methods, such as robustness and quality preservation, and manipulate large language models by either removing or spoofing watermarks, further exposing vulnerabilities in the detection systems and compromising the authenticity of AIGC \cite{pang2024attacking .}
elements.sent-2411-345: For image watermark forgery, an approach develops a watermark faker using adversarial learning to generate fake watermarked images, but it assumes attackers can access paired original and watermarked images, which is often unrealistic \cite{wang2021watermark .}
elements.sent-2411-346: However, current research on watermark forgery remains limited, indicating a need for more comprehensive studies to thoroughly assess the security of watermark against forgery.
elements.sent-2411-347: In watermark removal, attackers aim to erase the watermark hidden in the AIGC while preserving its visual quality.
elements.sent-2411-348: These attacks specifically target the watermarked content, meaning that $x_i$, $v_i$, and $o'_i$ could potentially serve as attack surfaces.
elements.sent-2411-349: However, due to the limited capabilities, attackers typically only have access to the final output $y'$.
elements.sent-2411-350: As a result, existing attacks typically view the final output $y'$ as the sole attack surface.
elements.sent-2411-351: Current watermark removal attacks can be categorized into several types:.
elements.sent-2411-352: Regeneration attacks \cite{zhao2023generative, zhao2023invisible, he2024can : These methods focus on regenerating images or text to eliminate watermarks.}
elements.sent-2411-353: Adversarial attacks \cite{jiang2023evading : These techniques manipulate the content using adversarial perturbations to evade detection.}
elements.sent-2411-354: Green token minimization attacks \cite{wu2024bypassing : This category specifically targets text watermarks, where attackers aim to minimize the presence of green tokens that signify the presence of a watermark.}
elements.sent-2411-355: Editing attacks: This category includes various editing techniques, for example, JPEG compression for images.
elements.sent-2411-356: For image watermark removal, utilizing an adversarial optimization strategy can evade detection in both white-box and black-box scenarios effectively \cite{jiang2023evading .}
elements.sent-2411-357: Random walk achieves successful watermark removal by reducing detection accuracy through iterative modifications \cite{zhang2023watermarks .}
elements.sent-2411-358: Adversarial methods targeting spectral traces within watermarked images can also effectively disrupt various watermarking schemes, though this may result in slight degradation of visual quality \cite{kassis2024unmarker .}
elements.sent-2411-359: Moreover, Zhao et al.~\cite{zhao2023generative, zhao2023invisible present a regeneration attack that targets invisible watermarks by degrading watermarked images into embeddings, adding random noise to disrupt the watermark, and then reconstructing the image from the altered embeddings.}
elements.sent-2411-360: In the realm of text watermark removal, He et al. propose the Cross-lingual Watermark Removal Attack (CWRA), which translates watermarked text to a pivot language and back, reducing watermark strength, with a limitation on the dependence of high-quality machine translation \cite{he2024can .}
elements.sent-2411-361: In addition, Wu et al. propose a method to evade watermark detection in LLM-generated text by prompting the LLM to generate text fragments that resemble human writing while minimizing green tokens, thus neutralizing the watermark \cite{wu2024bypassing .}
elements.sent-2411-362: Furthermore, Chang et al. present a watermark removal attack that uses a weaker reference LLM to infer watermarked tokens and smooth distribution perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-363: However, this approach relies on multiple queries to the watermarked model, which may be costly or impractical in some scenarios \cite{chang2024watermark .}
elements.sent-2411-364: Zhang et al. propose a method to remove watermarks from LLM-generated text by leveraging watermark detection rules and modeling green list theft as a mixed integer programming problem \cite{zhang2024large .}
elements.sent-2411-365: Among all those attack types, we believe regeneration attacks show significant promise due to their less stringent assumptions about attackers' capabilities and their demonstrated effectiveness in removing watermarks with minimal perturbations to the content.
elements.sent-2411-366: However, current regeneration attacks perform poorly against certain watermark methods \cite{fernandez2022watermarking, tancik2020stegastamp , which highlights a critical area for future research and improvement.}
elements.subsection-2411-020: Future Work on AIGC Watermark Security
elements.sent-2411-367: Despite the growing interest in watermarking technologies, research targeting the security of watermark remains limited.
elements.sent-2411-368: Thus, we believe the field is still in its nascent stages.
elements.sent-2411-369: In this section, we propose several potential research directions that could inspire the future development of watermark attacks, paving the way for more robust and secure watermarking solutions.
elements.subsubsection-2411-031: More Complex Defense for Endogenous Watermarks
elements.sent-2411-370: Different from traditional watermarks, endogeneity has become mainstream to embed watermarks into AIGC.
elements.sent-2411-371: However, this may introduce more complex defense update mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-372: Instead of directly modifying the small watermark module, the defense update for endogenous watermarks probably modifies the whole generative model whose overhead is unacceptable, especially for large models.
elements.sent-2411-373: Hence, it is essential to study more low-cost defense mechanisms for endogenous watermarks.
elements.sent-2411-374: Most existing watermark attacks and defenses target images or text.
elements.sent-2411-375: However, as the progress of cross-modality in generative models, especially multi-modality interaction in large models, their security remains unknown.
elements.sent-2411-376: Advanced LLMs like GPT-4o introduce HER mode, which allows users to employ both text and voice to interact with the model.
elements.sent-2411-377: Underlying the interactions, the tokens for text and voice are fused to produce outputs, introducing new attack surfaces to the corresponding watermarking modules that need further study.
elements.sent-2411-378: Most existing watermark attacks \cite{zhao2023generative, zhao2023invisible fail when confronted with semantic watermarking \cite{wen2023tree .}}
elements.sent-2411-379: This gap indicates a critical area for future study, as the robustness of semantic watermarks remains largely untested.
elements.sent-2411-380: Addressing the vulnerabilities of semantic watermarking should be a priority for researchers, as understanding and developing attack methods could lead to improved watermarking techniques and enhanced security measures.
elements.sent-2411-381: The widespread adoption of AIGC services raises concerns regarding the safety and trustworthiness of AI-generated content and draws significant attention to the governance of such services from countries and organizations.
elements.sent-2411-382: In this section, we first introduce the AIGC regulations from the aspect of various countries, followed by a more detailed examination of AIGC regulation from the perspective of five key elements of AIGC watermarking, dataset, user, generative model, AI-generated content, and watermark.
elements.sent-2411-383: Finally, we will discuss the future of AIGC governance.
elements.sent-2411-384: Recently, countries and organizations around the world have begun to formulate laws and regulations to govern AIGC and related services.
elements.sent-2411-385: The United States has released Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights \cite{blueprint , which emphasizes issues of fairness, transparency, privacy protection, and the development of AI.}
elements.sent-2411-386: Besides, The AI Risk Management Framework \cite{riskframework aims to regulate the use of AI, while the Malicious Deep Fake Prohibition Act \cite{MaliciousAct seeks to prohibit the malicious use of deepfake technology.}}
elements.sent-2411-387: Despite the regulations above, the United States has not yet proposed a legal framework specifically for AIGC.
elements.sent-2411-388: The European Union has passed the EU Artificial Intelligence Act \cite{euact , which addresses the safety, ethics, and fairness of AI, emphasizing the impact of AI technology on privacy rights and personal data, and focusing on the protection of users and consumers.}
elements.sent-2411-389: China places significant emphasis on the control of AIGC, having released a series of regulations \cite{gccontentregulation,draft and a compulsory standard \cite{GB for AIGC watermarking.}}
elements.sent-2411-390: China focuses more on the impact of AIGC on social governance and public safety, with stronger scrutiny and restrictions regarding the ethical and moral aspects of AIGC.
elements.sent-2411-391: Besides, low-quality data that is ambitious, violating, biased, or politically sensitive needs to be filtered before being used.
elements.sent-2411-392: Cross-border data flow: Singapore and China have restrictions \cite{PDPA,PIPL on the storage of related data to be stored entirely within the country's borders.}
elements.sent-2411-393: Several regulations have called for AI model structures and algorithms to be transparent to the public to foster trust and accountability.
elements.sent-2411-394: While open-sourcing models can drive technological innovation and ensure transparency, they also raise concerns about the potential misuse of these models by malicious individuals \cite{doi:10.1126/science.adp1848 .}
elements.sent-2411-395: AIGC tracing: Watermarks need to be capable of tracing the identity of users in cases where AIGC is misused \cite{euact,GB .}
elements.sent-2411-396: Some countries also require that user identity tracing methods must be robust enough to withstand potential attacks.
elements.sent-2411-397: Some countries especially stress the legal applications in terms of politics or religion \cite{euact,Ecological .}
elements.sent-2411-398: Spreading AI-generated false or misleading content also carries legal risks.
elements.sent-2411-399: Intellectual protection: The intellectual property rights of AIGC remain controversial, where regulations in different countries hold different interpretations.
elements.sent-2411-400: It is still hard to determine the ownership of AIGC copyrights, which should belong to the source data owner, the model developer, the AI itself, or the AIGC service users \cite{euact .}
elements.sent-2411-401: The regulations and laws currently issued by various countries have begun to outline the future landscape of AIGC governance.
elements.sent-2411-402: However, the formulation of AIGC regulation still faces numerous challenges.
elements.sent-2411-403: For instance, issues related to intellectual property—specifically, how to define whether AI-generated content belongs to the user or the AI developer—remain unresolved.
elements.sent-2411-404: Additionally, the question of liability—how responsibility should be attributed when AIGC leads to issues such as infringement or defamation—has yet to be clearly defined.
elements.sent-2411-405: Furthermore, due to differences in regulatory approaches, values, technical understanding, and legal systems across countries, there is a lack of a unified global regulatory framework for AIGC, which hinders the innovation and development of AI techniques.
elements.sent-2411-406: Watermark flows in the supply chain (of AIGC).
elements.sent-2411-407: We call for zooming out the scope of watermarking from the content itself to the supply chain, and thus see its impact on the entire system.
elements.sent-2411-408: We provide a formal definition of the AIGC watermark to distinguish it from the traditional watermark.
elements.sent-2411-409: We span the discussion of functionality and security of AIGC watermark from the perspective of AIGC watermark properties.
elements.sent-2411-410: We encourage the community to comprehensively explore the properties of the AIGC watermark and find the potential research directions.
elements.sent-2411-411: We hope that this work serves as a summary of current AIGC watermarks and boosts the research in the field of AIGC watermarks.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran { .}}
